stories.title: New York City Hotels Are Still Struggling to Regain Pre-Pandemic Numbers
stories.excerpt: The New York City hotels and hospitality sector is among the strongest in the nation, but it still hasn’t recovered to meet its pre-pandemic occupancy and revenue metrics. This is the conclusion drawn by New York State Comptroller Thomas DiNapoli in his annual study of New York City’s hotel industry, which found that while domestic visitors have surged, international visitors remain lower than their pre-pandemic benchmarks, contributing to lower pre-pandemic occupancy, employment and room rates. “The city’s hotel industry has largely recovered from the pandemic. It is doing well, but it could be doing even better if international visitors returned more quickly,” DiNapoli said in the report released this week. “Despite the challenges, we expect the sector to continue to grow, but a complete recovery requires a return of international visitors and increased employment to keep up with demand.” Of the 65 million visitors New York City hosted in 2025, 52.4 million were domestic tourists, while international visitors only accounted for 12.5 million, which was down from 2024 and roughly 93 percent of its pre-pandemic numbers, according to DiNapoli. Moreover, while the city’s 84 percent hotel occupancy rate was the highest in the nation for the third straight year, the city’s occupancy metric remained 3.5 percent below its 2019 number. Furthermore, the $333.71 average daily room rate remained lower than its pre-pandemic average adjusted for inflation. Perhaps most concerning, the New York City hotel industry today employs 45,325 men and women, which is 13 percent fewer than it employed in 2019. “The comptroller’s report confirms what the hotel industry has been saying for years: We have yet to recover from the pandemic, and we are still thousands of jobs and millions of visitors below where we were,” said Vijay Dandapani, president and CEO of the Hotel Association of New York City, a trade group that represents 250 city hotels. That said, it’s not all doom and gloom. The nu
bullets: NYC hosted 65M visitors in 2025; only 12.5M international (down YoY, 93% of pre-pandemic).
Hotel occupancy, employment, room rates still below pre-pandemic despite strong domestic surge.
State Comptroller: full recovery requires international visitor return and employment growth.
stories.summary: New York City's hotel sector has recovered broadly but lags pre-pandemic benchmarks, with occupancy and room rates held back by weak international travel. Of 65 million 2025 visitors, 52.4 million were domestic; international arrivals of 12.5 million fell short of 2024 and remain at roughly 93% of pre-pandemic levels. State officials expect ongoing sector growth but signal that complete recovery hinges on international traveler rebound.
stories.impact: Weak international tourism signals sustained softness in NYC's white-collar office markets and luxury multifamily absorption. Delayed business travel and international relocation dampens prime office tenant demand and high-end residential leasing in Manhattan, likely keeping cap rates elevated and limiting rent growth in luxury segments through 2026.

stories.title: Catholic Homes Secures $147M Construction Loan for Bronx Housing Development
stories.excerpt: An affiliate of the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New York just secured construction financing for a housing development at 484 East 178th Street in the Bronx’s Tremont neighborhood. Catholic Homes, a nonprofit dedicated to developing and managing affordable housing for families and seniors, received a $146.9 million construction loan from the New York State Housing Finance Agency to bring 251 new units to the site, according to property records. The site at the corner of East 178th Street and Washington Avenue is currently a vacant lot. Monsignor Kevin Nelan was the signatory for Catholic Homes. The organization did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. Catholic Homes acquired the development site earlier this year for $17 million from another church organization, the Roman Catholic Church of Saint Simon Stock and Saint Joseph. The NYS Housing Finance Agency — which also did not immediately respond to a request for comment — was founded in 1960 to provide financing for low- and moderate-income rental multifamily housing. Catholic Homes develops and preserves properties across New York City, with the goal of providing aid to poor and vulnerable individuals and families. All told, Catholic Homes has provided New Yorkers with 481 senior housing units, 2,350 family housing units and 128 supportive housing units. The organization has several housing and supportive developments across the city, including Clinton Broome at 157 and 165 Broome Street in Manhattan’s Lower East Side, the George Hardy at 440 East 137th Street in the Bronx’s Mott Haven neighborhood, and the Carmel Apartments at 61 Carmel Court in Staten Island. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Catholic Homes secures $146.9M construction loan from NYS Housing Finance Agency, Bronx Tremont.
251-unit affordable housing development at 484 E. 178th St.; site acquired for $17M earlier this year.
Vacant lot conversion; nonprofit positioning for senior and family-focused rental in outer borough.
stories.summary: Catholic Homes, an affiliate of the Roman Catholic Archdiocese, closed a $146.9 million construction loan from the New York State Housing Finance Agency to develop 251 units of affordable multifamily housing on a vacant parcel in the Bronx's Tremont neighborhood. The nonprofit acquired the site in early 2025 for $17 million from another church entity and aims to serve families and seniors. The deal signals continued institutional capital flow toward affordable housing in underutilized Bronx submarkets.
stories.impact: Demonstrates sustained HFA lending appetite for outer-borough affordable development, creating pipeline pressure on market-rate rents in Bronx Transit-Oriented pockets like Tremont. Capital flowing to nonprofit affordable production limits land availability and raises land costs for for-profit developers, particularly in walkable subway corridors where gentrification risk is highest.

stories.title: Mamdani Releases Action Plan to Tackle Rental Ripoff Hearing Findings
stories.excerpt: Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s administration released a report Thursday morning detailing what it gleaned from the so-called Rental Ripoff hearings and what it plans to do about some of the most pervasive issues experienced by tenants and landlords. Complaints expressed during the hearings didn’t stray far from expectations, with 16 percent of the 2,400 participants complaining of pest infestations, as well as mold, heat, hot water issues and broken elevators. But many tenants also expressed a sense of fear of retaliation from landlords and building managers for voicing grievances. Cea Weaver, director of the Mayor’s Office to Protect Tenants, briefed reporters on the findings in the report Wednesday and discussed measures to hold bad landlords accountable while exploring new options to take control of problematic buildings and portfolios. “The recommendations [in the report], some of them are administrative actions that we can take as a city,” Weaver said during the briefing. “Some of them are legislative actions that we will need to work with the City Council to make a reality. And some of them require court action or litigation. So we’re going to be rolling these out over the next few years. This is not something that we’re going to be able to implement in the report tomorrow.” One of the ways the Mamdani administration plans to tackle complaints is by requiring that inspectors at least attempt to launch an investigation on every complaint, starting in October with the upcoming building heating season. If there are multiple complaints from different apartments within the same building, they will no longer be bunched together into one complaint, but will be treated as individual potential violations, according to the report. Audience members cheer and listen as Mayor Zohran Mamdani speaks at a “Rental Ripoff” hearing at Fordham University in the Bronx in March. PHOTO: Spencer Platt/Getty Images The city will also crack down on false self-cer
bullets: NYC Mayor Mamdani releases action plan from 'Rental Ripoff' hearings; 2,400 participants surveyed.
16% complained of pest/mold/heat; widespread tenant fear of retaliation from landlords.
City exploring new enforcement, lease protection, and potential building acquisition/control measures.
stories.summary: New York City's Office to Protect Tenants released findings from spring 'Rental Ripoff' hearings attended by 2,400 residents. Common complaints included pest infestations, mold, heating failures, and broken elevators; tenants also expressed fear of retaliation for reporting issues. The city's action plan includes both administrative enforcement and legislative proposals to hold landlords accountable and explore acquisition of problem buildings and portfolios.
stories.impact: Escalating tenant protections and landlord enforcement in NYC increase operating compliance costs and reputational risk for multifamily owners, narrowing NOI margins on smaller, older stock. Risk of city intervention (building seizure, rent-control expansion, mandatory capital spend orders) pressures valuations and slows investor appetite for value-add repositioning in outer boroughs where compliance deficiencies are highest.

stories.title: San Antonio’s Aztec Hotel Reveals Open Date
stories.excerpt: It’s taken 10 years for the developers of a River Walk boutique hotel to reach the point where they can announce their opening date. The San Antonio Business Journal reports the Aztec Hotel, at the site of the Aztec Theatre building, will open near the end of August. The owners of the downtown property, led by Sam Panchevre, are nearing completion of the project to transform a portion of the structure into a new boutique hotel. The redevelopment of multiple floors into a new River Walk hotel is now more than 90% complete. The project has survived historic preservation hurdles, a global pandemic and economic instability. Panchevre’s group has teamed with Shreem Capital, which will co-own and manage the new 77-room hotel and fly the Marriott flag. A river-level speakeasy and rooftop terrace with a restaurant and bar will open at roughly the same time as the hotel. The post San Antonio’s Aztec Hotel Reveals Open Date appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Aztec Hotel, San Antonio River Walk redevelopment, opens late August after 10-year timeline.
77-room boutique hotel; Marriott flag; speakeasy and rooftop restaurant/bar amenities included.
Survived historic preservation, pandemic, economic headwinds; Shreem Capital co-owns and manages.
stories.summary: The Aztec Hotel, a 77-room boutique redevelopment of the historic Aztec Theatre in downtown San Antonio's River Walk, will open in late August after a decade-long entitlement and construction cycle. Co-owned and managed by Shreem Capital under the Marriott brand, the project includes a river-level speakeasy and rooftop restaurant/bar. The long timeline reflects San Antonio's historic preservation and zoning complexity in high-visibility waterfront districts.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development; hospitality conversion signals demand for River Walk experiences but does not materially affect residential supply or rental demand in San Antonio's broader multifamily markets.

stories.title: Intercontinental Real Estate Corporation Acquires Grocery-Anchored Retail Center in Auburn for $70M
stories.excerpt: Boston-based Intercontinental Real Estate Corporation has acquired Lakeland Town Center, a 125,233-square-foot grocery-anchored retail center located in Auburn, Washington, for $69.5 million. Geoff Tranchina, Gleb Lvovich, Dan Tyner, and Tess Berghoff with JLL Capital Markets marketed the property on behalf of the seller. Built in 2002, Lakeland Town Center is situated on a 12.61-acre site at the commercial heart of Lakeland Hills. Lakeland Center is 100% leased and is anchored by regional grocer Haggen Northwest Fresh. “Lakeland Town Center is the dominant daily-needs destination in a captive, high-income trade area, supported by a top-performing grocery anchor and one of the tightest retail submarkets in the Seattle metro,” said Jessica Levin, Managing Director, West Coast and Associate CIO at Intercontinental. The property consists of both pad and inline retail with 704 parking spaces. “This acquisition reflects our focus on well-located, grocery-anchored retail with strong demand fundamentals,” added Ross Karetsky, Associate Director, Acquisitions at Intercontinental. The post Intercontinental Real Estate Corporation Acquires Grocery-Anchored Retail Center in Auburn for $70M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Intercontinental Real Estate acquires Lakeland Town Center, Auburn, Washington for $69.5M.
125K SF, 100% leased, grocery-anchored (Haggen NW Fresh); 12.6-acre site in high-income Seattle suburb.
Reflects investor appetite for well-located, tight-supply retail submarkets with strong daily-needs anchor.
stories.summary: Boston-based Intercontinental Real Estate purchased Lakeland Town Center, a 125,233-square-foot fully leased grocery-anchored retail center in Auburn, Washington, for $69.5 million. Built in 2002 and anchored by regional grocer Haggen Northwest Fresh, the 12.61-acre property sits in a high-income trade area with one of the Pacific Northwest's tightest retail submarkets. Intercontinental's acquisition reflects continued capital strength in Seattle-area essential retail.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development; retail acquisition in suburban Seattle does not directly signal residential demand or supply shifts, though tight retail submarkets may correlate with constrained land availability and rising development costs in Auburn and surrounding South King County markets.

stories.title: Taiwan Semiconductor Adding Another $100B to Phoenix Chip Making Effort
stories.excerpt: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC) plans to spend an extra $100 billion to grow its U.S. business. The company says that money will likely help add four new fabrication plants in Arizona, which would make some of the world’s most advanced computer chips, 2-nanometer and smaller. Those new factories would be added to TSMC’s big Arizona project in the North Valley. The company has already announced a $165 billion expansion that includes six chip plants, two advanced packaging facilities and a research and development center. The site is south of Loop 303 and west of Interstate 17. All together, the company’s total promised investment in U.S. chipmaking now looks like roughly $265 billion. TSMC makes chips used by big-name companies like Nvidia and Apple. The company says the big push is happening because more and more people and businesses want AI, and AI needs powerful computer chips. Earlier this year, TSMC paid nearly $200 million for land south of its sprawling campus near 43rd Avenue and Dove Valley Road in northwest Phoenix. The post Taiwan Semiconductor Adding Another $100B to Phoenix Chip Making Effort appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: TSMC committing additional $100B to U.S. chipmaking, bringing total U.S. investment to ~$265B.
Four new fab plants planned for Phoenix area (North Valley, south of Loop 303/west of I-17).
Expansion driven by AI demand; company already owns ~$200M land south of 43rd Ave/Dove Valley Rd.
stories.summary: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. announced an additional $100 billion investment in U.S. operations, raising its total committed U.S. capital to approximately $265 billion. The funding will support four new advanced chip fabrication plants in Arizona's North Valley, built atop an already-announced $165 billion program including six fabs, two packaging facilities, and an R&D center. TSMC's expansion is driven by surging AI chip demand and its role as supplier to Nvidia and Apple.
stories.impact: TSMC's massive Phoenix capex influx will accelerate AI-sector job and population migration into metropolitan Phoenix, particularly north of the Loop 303 corridor and west of I-17. This unlocks strong multifamily demand in North Valley submarkets (Glendale, Surprise, Peoria) and secondary employment hubs over the next 5–7 years; developers should acquire land and underwrite projects now ahead of lease-up acceleration in 2027–2029.

stories.title: Affinius Provides $177M Construction Loan for Central Florida Student Housing
stories.excerpt: Student housing continues to secure institutional capital. Gideon Friedman’s Beachwold Residential has secured $176.6 million of construction financing to build the first two phases of Place at Alafaya, a four-building 1,395-bed student housing property near the University of Central Florida campus in Orlando, Commercial Observer can first report. Affinius Capital provided the construction debt, while JLL’s Mona Carlton, Elliott Throne and Josh Odessky arranged the transaction. The financing is the largest private residential construction loan in Orlando’s history, according to JLL. Tyler Figley, senior vice president at Affinius Capital, said in a statement that the new student housing property will benefit from its location near the campus and the growing student enrollment at UCF. “This development aligns with our strategy of financing high-quality student housing with top-tier sponsorship at leading universities,” added Figley. Located at 11600 MacKay Boulevard — just west of North Alafaya Trail and a 15 minute walk from UCF campus — the first phase of Place at Alafaya will deliver two buildings featuring 284 units with just over 800 beds of student housing. Construction for this section will include 767 parking spaces and be finished in 2028, while the second phase will deliver a 200-unit, 585-bed property and 534 parking spaces by 2029. The complex also includes a three-story, 16,000-square-foot clubhouse that will feature amenities such as a coffee bar, social and study lounges, a theater, and a fitness and wellness center, along with courtyards, cabanas, pickleball courts, a basketball court and a dog park. Brian Pascus can be reached at bpascus@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Affinius Capital provides $176.6M construction loan for Beachwold Residential student housing, Orlando.
Place at Alafaya: 1,395 beds across four buildings near UCF campus; largest private residential construction loan in Orlando history.
Strong student enrollment growth at UCF supports leasing fundamentals and institutional capital appetite.
stories.summary: Beachwold Residential secured $176.6 million in construction financing from Affinius Capital for Place at Alafaya, a 1,395-bed, four-building student housing development adjacent to the University of Central Florida campus. The deal, arranged by JLL, is the largest private residential construction loan in Orlando's history. Affinius cited UCF's rising student enrollment and the property's proximity to campus as core underwriting drivers.
stories.impact: Strong institutional capital deployment to UCF-area student housing reflects tight supply and sustained enrollment growth at one of Florida's largest state universities. Success of this mega-deal signals investor confidence in Central Florida's demographic tailwinds and positions aggressive student housing investors to compress pricing in the submarket; non-student multifamily operators should monitor UCF-area rent growth and occupancy to assess spillover rental demand in nearby off-campus rental markets.

stories.title: West Palm Beach Eyeing Waterfront Rezoning Moratorium
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach gave tentative approval to a measure that would pause redevelopment in a waterfront area of the city’s downtown. It will mostly affect the seven apartment and condo projects on S. Flagler Drive and Washington Road. The S. Florida Business Journal reports the measure would halt any new projects in the area that require rezoning. Developments filed earlier wouldn’t be affected. The measure comes amid growing community pushback against the scale and speed of redevelopment in West Palm Beach’s urban core in recent years. The six-month pause would give the city time to hire an outside consultant to recommend potential zoning changes to the area, which is home to a number of vintage multifamily and condominium buildings (shown) that could be ripe for luxury redevelopment. Before the moratorium can go into effect, the ordinance would require a second reading by city commissioners. That vote is slated for July 20. Stay ahead of the multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Gain firsthand insights from industry leaders on capital markets, development, operations, financing, and investment trends while expanding your network with the people making deals happen. See you on September 22! The post West Palm Beach Eyeing Waterfront Rezoning Moratorium appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: West Palm Beach tentatively approves six-month rezoning moratorium on S. Flagler Drive/Washington Road waterfront area.
Moratorium affects seven apartment/condo projects; aims to slow rapid downtown redevelopment pace.
City hiring consultant for zoning analysis; second reading vote scheduled July 20; expires January 20, 2027.
stories.summary: West Palm Beach's city commission gave tentative approval to a six-month moratorium on new rezoning applications in a waterfront corridor spanning S. Flagler Drive and Washington Road, affecting approximately seven planned apartment and condo projects. The pause, pending final commission vote on July 20, reflects community pushback against rapid downtown redevelopment. The city is hiring a consultant to recommend zoning changes; the moratorium is set to expire January 20, 2027, unless extended.
stories.impact: Moratorium creates regulatory uncertainty and delays capital deployment in West Palm Beach's highest-velocity redevelopment corridor, pressuring sponsors to accelerate entitlements before January 2027 or defer projects. Vintage multifamily and condo buildings on Flagler—prime conversion/demolition candidates—face frozen development status, potentially steepening land prices for developers with pre-moratorium approvals and dampening competitive bidding for long-held assemblies.

stories.title: Peerless Building 259-Unit NCSU Student Housing Development
stories.excerpt: Peerless Development is pursuing the construction of a 713-bed student housing project on Crest Road directly behind North Carolina State University’s South Campus. The Triangle Business Journal reports that the site plans show a 7-story mixed-use building — the height would range from 5 to 7 stories due to grade changes on the site. There will be 259 units in total, with 50 one-bedroom units, 80 two-bedroom units, 23 three-bedroom units, 96 four-bedroom units, and 10 five-bedroom units. The project will be two levels of underground parking with 294 parking spaces. The only amenity detailed in the plans is a swimming pool. Peerless expects to add a retail component to the project. The site is 2.57 acres at 1523 Crest Road, the current location of an apartment complex (shown), which must be razed before construction begins. Peerless Development specializes in multifamily, student housing and industrial development and acquisition. The post Peerless Building 259-Unit NCSU Student Housing Development appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Peerless Development planning 259-unit, 713-bed student housing on Crest Road behind NCSU South Campus, Raleigh.
7-story mixed-use with underground parking (294 spaces); retail component TBD; existing apartment complex to be razed.
Unit mix: 50 one-BR, 80 two-BR, 23 three-BR, 96 four-BR, 10 five-BR; emphasizes shared-bed supply.
stories.summary: Peerless Development is planning a 259-unit, 713-bed student housing project directly behind North Carolina State University's South Campus on Crest Road in Raleigh. The mixed-use 5–7 story building will include two levels of underground parking (294 spaces), a swimming pool, and undisclosed retail. Unit mix emphasizes shared-bedroom configurations (four- and five-bedroom units comprise 41% of units). Existing apartment stock at the site will be demolished.
stories.impact: NCSU student housing pipeline expansion in Raleigh signals sustained enrollment and rent growth; Peerless's aggressive bed-count focus (2.75 beds/unit) indicates sponsor confidence in shared-lease demand and ability to command premium rents from upper-class cohorts. Competing multifamily operators near campus should monitor absorption and rent trajectory to assess spillover pressure on older off-campus student rental stock and general Raleigh market tightening.

stories.title: Center for Justice Innovation Expands to 34K SF at GFP’s 520 Eighth Avenue
stories.excerpt: The Center for Justice Innovation, a nonprofit organization focused on community safety and racial justice, is taking more space at its Midtown offices. The nonprofit has signed a deal to expand to 34,300 square feet at GFP Real Estate’s 520 Eighth Avenue and extend its current lease at the property, the landlord announced Thursday. The 20-year lease spans a portion of the 18th floor and the entire 19th floor of the 26-story Midtown office building. The Center for Justice Innovation has been a tenant at the property since 1999 and has expanded within the building over the years, most recently occupying 33,000 square feet on parts of the 10th, 18th and 23rd floors. This new lease gives the company an extra 1,300 square feet and consolidates the organization’s footprint in the building over two floors. “Center for Justice Innovation has been part of the 520 Eighth Avenue community for more than 25 years, and we’re proud to continue supporting the organization through this next phase of growth,” GFP’s Matthew Mandell, who represented the landlord in-house, said in a statement. “This transaction further reinforces 520 Eighth Avenue’s position as one of Manhattan’s premier hubs for mission driven organizations.” The asking rent was not disclosed, but the average asking rent for office properties in the Penn District was $70.09 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to research from Newmark. Open Impact Real Estate’s Stephen Powers and Jake Cinti represented the tenant in the deal. “As the role of the workplace continues to evolve, nonprofits are increasingly approaching real estate as a strategic tool to deepen impact and support long-term sustainability,” Cinti said in a statement. “By consolidating the Center for Justice Innovation’s operations into a modernized and highly efficient headquarters, this transaction not only positions the organization for continued growth and long-term savings, but creates a workplace environment that reflects
bullets: Center for Justice Innovation expands to 34.3K SF at GFP Real Estate's 520 Eighth Ave, Midtown Manhattan.
20-year lease extension; nonprofit consolidates from fragmented 33K SF across three floors to cohesive 18th–19th floor footprint.
Long-term tenant retention signals stable nonprofit office demand and landlord willingness to offer continuity pricing.
stories.summary: The Center for Justice Innovation, a nonprofit focused on community safety and racial justice, signed a 20-year lease extension to expand to 34,300 square feet at GFP Real Estate's 520 Eighth Avenue in Midtown Manhattan. The organization consolidates from 33,000 square feet scattered across the 10th, 18th, and 23rd floors to occupy the entire 18th floor and part of the 19th floor, gaining an additional 1,300 square feet. The nonprofit has been a 520 Eighth Avenue tenant since 1999.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development; nonprofit office expansion signals modest Midtown Class B office demand and tenant permanence but does not materially affect residential market supply, demand, or rent dynamics in Manhattan.

stories.title: Miami secures $193M permit to build civic center at Freedom Park
stories.excerpt: The city of Miami’s new civic center, which will replace an aging administrative building downtown and the city’s iconic city hall in Coconut Grove, secured a $193 million construction permit, the city confirmed. The city received its permit July 1,  just over a week before construction workers topped off the eight-story, 257,000-square-foot building on July 10. Adler Development is the developer, and Moss Construction is the general contractor.  The building will be part of the 130-acre Miami Freedom Park development, which is a $1 billion project anchored by Inter Miami CF’s stadium near Miami International Airport. The project, which includes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami secures $193M construction permit for new civic center at Freedom Park; building topped off July 10.
257K SF, eight-story city administrative hub replacing downtown office and Coconut Grove City Hall (70+ years).
Part of $1B mixed-use Freedom Park anchored by Inter Miami CF stadium; $20M Metrorail pedestrian bridge; completion spring 2028.
stories.summary: The City of Miami received a $193 million construction permit for its new 257,000-square-foot civic center at Freedom Park, with topping-off completed July 10. The eight-story building will consolidate city administrative functions from downtown and the 1954 City Hall landmark in Coconut Grove, ending more than 70 years of governance at the Art Deco Pan Am building. The civic center is part of the $1 billion, 130-acre Miami Freedom Park mixed-use development anchored by Inter Miami CF's stadium; project completion is targeted for spring 2028.
stories.impact: City investment in Freedom Park civic infrastructure accelerates broader mixed-use development momentum near Miami International Airport, increasing long-term office, retail, and entertainment employment density and supporting multifamily demand in south-central Miami and nearby neighborhoods. Developers should monitor Freedom Park's phased openings (2027–2028) and Inter Miami's tenant draw to forecast residential absorption and rent growth in submarkets within 2–3 miles.

stories.title: Santa Monica, elevated: Related sets new standard for SoCal luxury with lifestyle amenities, Thermador appliances
stories.excerpt: Today’s luxury buyer isn’t just looking for fancy finishes and great views; they’re expecting a residence that facilitates their lifestyle. In our latest Developer Diary, TRD visited 700 Broadway and sat down with Related California’s Peter Garofalo to learn how the developer created an exclusive luxury residential experience in one of SoCal’s most desirable submarkets. “The ideal resident for 700 Broadway is someone who wants to have it all in a wonderfully designed and luxurious setting,” said Garofalo. “Every space has been perfectly designed to keep a balance between the beachside ease and the city’s bustling energy.” With nearly 70,000 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related California delivers 700 Broadway luxury residential, Santa Monica; 70K SF amenity-centric design.
Three buildings (brick and glass), private courtyard (0.75 acres), rooftop pools/hot tubs/sauna; lifestyle-first positioning.
Thermador appliances, speakeasy bar, natural/handmade materials; targets affluent residents seeking elevated beachside experience.
stories.summary: Related California's 700 Broadway in Santa Monica is a three-building luxury residential development emphasizing lifestyle amenities over unit count. The project includes nearly 70,000 square feet of curated indoor and outdoor amenity space: a multi-acre private courtyard, rooftop pools, hot tubs, sauna barrels, a speakeasy bar, and recreational areas. Design philosophy blends beachside ease with urban energy; residences feature Thermador appliances and natural materials. The development targets affluent buyers seeking curated luxury experiences.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development strategy; this is a luxury condo/for-sale project, not rental. However, amenity-density trends and lifestyle positioning reflect broader Santa Monica market tightening and rising per-unit development costs, suggesting rental developers should expect higher construction and capital costs and increased value-capture expectations from coastal LA luxury projects.

stories.title: Moratorium on waterfront project applications in downtown West Palm heads to final vote
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach commissioners will take a final vote on pausing new project applications for high-rise waterfront developments at next week’s meeting.  The proposed ordinance would create a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for properties south of Monroe Drive, north of Southern Boulevard, west of Flagler Drive and east of Washington Road, according to the commission agenda. Commissioners approved it on first reading at a meeting last week; a second reading vote is scheduled for July 20.  The city cited rapid transformation of that corridor of apartment and condo buildings and is hiring Zyscovich to complete a zoning […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: West Palm Beach moratorium on high-rise waterfront applications (south Monroe to north Southern Blvd, W. Flagler to E. Washington).
Six-month pause expires January 20, 2027; city hiring Zyscovich for zoning/economic analysis and redevelopment recommendations.
12+ luxury condo projects totaling 2K+ units in works; Related, Terra, BH Group (Miami-based) driving development spree.
stories.summary: West Palm Beach commissioners approved a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for high-rise waterfront projects in downtown (south of Monroe, north of Southern Boulevard, between Flagler and Washington Road). The pause, effective through January 20, 2027, is a response to rapid downtown transformation and luxury condo development concentration. The city is hiring Zyscovich Architects to analyze zoning and redevelopment strategy. Over a dozen luxury condo projects totaling 2,000+ units are underway, with Related Group, Terra, and BH Group spearheading the boom.
stories.impact: Moratorium creates hard deadline pressure for sponsors with pending applications and applications filed before the freeze; expect filing surge through mid-July and potential bid-up of shovel-ready assets. Moratorium also signals political risk to future high-rise approvals and may incentivize shift toward rental (apartment) vs. condo supply; multifamily developers should position to acquire and fast-track entitled rental projects in the Q4 2025–Q1 2026 window before sentiment hardens further.

stories.title: MidPen floats 110-unit affordable housing project on former Wells Fargo site in Sunnyvale
stories.excerpt: Developer interest in downtown Sunnyvale continues with another multifamily project proposal as more tech and artificial intelligence workers flock to the Silicon Valley city.  Foster City-based nonprofit MidPen Housing is planning a 110-unit affordable housing building at 295 South Mathilda Avenue in Sunnyvale, the East Bay Times reported. All of the units, save for a market-rate manager’s unit, will be income restricted.  The development was proposed at a former Wells Fargo bank site, which would give way to the residential building. The development earned support from some residents and community groups who claim it “will assist the city by better […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MidPen Housing proposes 110-unit affordable building at former Wells Fargo site (295 S. Mathilda Ave), Sunnyvale.
All units save manager's unit income-restricted; 25% designated for previously unhoused individuals; Area Median Income ~$143.9K.
AI-driven tech worker inflow and office-to-residential conversions fueling Sunnyvale multifamily investment momentum.
stories.summary: Foster City-based nonprofit MidPen Housing is advancing a 110-unit affordable residential project on a vacant former Wells Fargo bank site in downtown Sunnyvale. All units except a market-rate manager's unit will be income-restricted; 25% will serve formerly unhoused individuals. County area median income is $143,850 (one-person household). The project has support from local advocacy groups and reflects broader multifamily investment surge in Sunnyvale driven by AI-sector growth and office-to-residential conversion interest.
stories.impact: Sunnyvale emerging as secondary multifamily hub for Bay Area AI and tech workers as San Francisco office-to-residential conversion and remote-work trends push demand south. Affordable housing at downtown Sunnyvale's core (near transit) signals strong market fundamentals and investor confidence; for-profit developers should accelerate acquisition in Sunnyvale's tech-corridor office parks and mixed-use districts to capture conversion and new-build opportunity ahead of zoning/entitlement tightening.

stories.title: Pair of Frisco entrepreneurs ordered to pay $400M in damages to Georgetown business over alleged fraud scheme
stories.excerpt: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered to pay $400 million in damages to a Georgetown-based business after being found liable for breach of contract and fraud by a Williamson County jury.  The pair concocted promotional materials for a luxury development called “The District” that they claimed was going to be built around the San Gabriel River. Krishnan and Gounder were sold the land by the company that sued them, Energy Commissioning Incorporated, owned by Marshall Hussain.  Energy Commissioning was subsequently hired by the pair for $21 million to build necessary infrastructure for a 400-acre, luxury waterfront development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan and Gounder liable for $400M damages; defrauded Georgetown business owner via fake 'District' waterfront development.
Scheme: 400-acre luxury River San Gabriel project never meant to materialize; $21M paid for infrastructure by duped partner.
Krishnan/Gounder previously targeted by SEC (2023) for alleged Ponzi scheme; never secured $5B bond.
stories.summary: A Williamson County jury found Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder liable for $400 million in damages to Georgetown-based Energy Commissioning Inc. for breach of contract and fraud. The pair marketed a fake 400-acre luxury waterfront development called 'The District' around the San Gabriel River and defrauded the company's owner, Marshall Hussain, by securing a $21 million construction contract for infrastructure that was never meant to materialize. Krishnan and Gounder made only symbolic payments and caused Hussain to lose his home. Both defendants were previously targeted by the SEC in 2023 for an alleged Ponzi scheme.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development; this is a fraud/litigation case involving real estate scheme operators. However, it signals reputational and legal risk in Central Texas waterfront development deals and underscores importance of counterparty due diligence, particularly with lesser-known developers pitching mega-scale mixed-use projects with vague financing structures.

stories.title: Bellhops and bass fishing: Wildhaven developer bets big on nature-centric luxury in Denton
stories.excerpt: A planned luxury resort might be coming to Denton, complete with six miles of horse trails to help bring residents and visitors closer to nature.  Sarasota, Florida-based An Ki Land Development’s managing director, Logan Breen, told the Dallas Business Journal that the new resort they plan on building is intended to combine upscale homes and hotels with “authentic outdoor experiences,” with a total value between $350 and $450 million when completed. The resort, called Wildhaven, is set to span 740 acres on the north side of Denton. 152 homes are set to be built in the resort, ranging from $1.5 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: An Ki Land Development (Sarasota) planning Wildhaven luxury resort, Denton, Texas; 740 acres, $350–450M total value.
152 homes ($1.5M–$3M+), 100-key hotel, restaurants, equestrian, spa, bass pond; 400 acres developed, 300+ preserved.
Noble Classic Homes building 20 homes in Phase 1; additional builders TBD; positioned as first Wildhaven lifestyle brand location.
stories.summary: An Ki Land Development is planning Wildhaven, a 740-acre luxury master-planned resort in Denton combining 152 upscale homes ($1.5M–$3M+ price points), a 100-key hotel, restaurants, equestrian facilities, spa, and a 10-acre bass fishing pond. Total project value is estimated at $350–450 million. Of 740 acres, 400 are slated for development and 300+ preserved for natural experience. Noble Classic Homes will build 20 homes in Phase 1; additional builders will be announced. Wildhaven is positioned as the flagship property in a planned brand expansion to Colorado and Florida.
stories.impact: Wildhaven signals strong investor appetite for luxury experiential/lifestyle-driven resort communities in DFW growth corridors north of Dallas, driven by Denton's rapid population and job growth. Successful execution could accelerate land development and presales velocity in North Texas exurban markets; multifamily operators should monitor Denton's absorption and pricing to assess spillover rental demand from luxury homebuyers seeking temporary housing during construction and from resort/hospitality employees.
